1: The twenty-year building summary — miqqēṣ ʿeśrîm šānāh (at the end of twenty years). miqqēṣ (H7093, from the end of) — the temporal hinge after which Solomon’s administrative and territorial expansion begins. The twenty years divides neatly: seven years for the temple (1 Kings 6:38) and thirteen years for the palace (1 Kings 7:1). The Chronicler does not preserve these numbers here, focusing instead on the building’s completion as a unified event.
2: waYYiḇen šlōmōh ʾeT-hāʿārîm — Solomon rebuilt the towns Huram had given him. In 1 Kings 9:10-13 the arrangement is reversed — Solomon gave Huram twenty cities, who found them unsatisfactory and called them ‘Cabul.’ The Chronicler’s version has Huram giving cities to Solomon. The divergence reflects either different source traditions or the Chronicler’s ideological adjustment: a Solomon who receives from the Gentile king rather than gives away Israelite territory better fits the Chronicler’s presentation of Solomon’s imperial success.
3: waYYēleḵ šlōmōh ḥămāT-ṣôḇāh — Solomon marched to Hamath-zobah and captured it. Hamath-zobah is the Aramean kingdom north of Damascus. This military action is unique to Chronicles; Samuel-Kings does not record it. The Chronicler may draw on a source otherwise lost. The verb yālaḵ (went/marched) with waYYiLKaDâH (and captured it) presents Solomon as not merely a builder but a military commander — the Solomonic empire extends to the northern frontier.
4: waYYiḇen ʾeT-tadMōr bamMidBār — he built Tadmor in the desert. Tadmor is Palmyra, the famous desert oasis city in the Syrian desert. The Chronicler includes this as part of Solomon’s store-city building programme in the far north. The building of store cities (ʿārê miSkenôT) reflects the administrative infrastructure of the empire: food reserves, tax collection, and supply chains for military and trade purposes.
5: waYYiḇen ʾeT-bêT-ḥôrôn hāʿelyônāh wĕʾeT-bêT-ḥôrôn haṭTaḥtônāh — Upper and Lower Beth-horon. Beth-horon was the key pass controlling the Shephelah-to-highlands route; both fortified by Solomon (ʿārîm bĕṣûrôT, fortified cities) with walls, gates, and bars (ḥômâh dĕlātayim ûḇərîaḥ). The same Beth-horon appeared in 7:24 as built by Sheerah. Solomon now fortifies what Ephraim’s daughter had built — the administrative continuity of strategic locations across Israel’s history.
6: bāʿalāT (Baalath) — a Danite town (Joshua 19:44; 1 Kings 9:18). The store cities and chariot cities (ʿārê hāreḵeḇ) and cavalry cities (ʿārê hapPārāšîm) represent the infrastructure of Solomon’s military power. ʾēt kol-ḥēšeq šĕlōmōh (everything Solomon desired/was pleased to build) — ḥēšeq (H2836, desire/pleasure) — the same root as the covenant-desire vocabulary (Deuteronomy 7:7: not because of your greatness did YHWH ḥāšaq to you). Solomon’s building desires are limitless — in Lebanon, throughout his territory.
7: The non-Israelite peoples — haḥittî wĕhāʾĕmōrî wĕhapPĕrizzî wĕhaḥiWwî wĕhaYYĕḇûsî — the standard list of Canaan’s pre-Israelite inhabitants. ʾăšer lōʾ-mēyiśrāʾēl hēmmāh (who were not from Israel) — the ethnic distinction between the forced labor pool and the Israelite population. These are the nôter (H3498, remainder/survivors) from the incomplete conquest — the peoples Israel failed to drive out who now serve the Solomonic state.
8: waYYaʿălēm šĕlōmōh lĕmas — Solomon conscripted them as forced labor (mas, H4522). The mas system (corvée/tribute labor) was the standard ANE mechanism for mobilizing labor for large building projects — attested in Mesopotamian and Egyptian administrative texts. ʿad-haYYôm hazZeh (until this day) — a formulaic phrase indicating the information comes from a source contemporary with conditions the Chronicler knew had ended or changed.
9: ûmibBĕnê yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-nātan šĕlōmōh ʿăḇādîm — Solomon did not make Israelites as slaves. ʿeḇed (H5650, slave/servant) — the distinction is fundamental: Israelites serve as ʾanšê milḥāmāh (warriors/soldiers), śārê šālîšāyw (officers), śārê rĕḵeḇ (chariot commanders), and pārāšîm (cavalry). The covenant people are free men in the military hierarchy; the non-Israelites are the labor pool. The same distinction the Exodus narrative established — Israel freed from Egyptian slave-labor (mas, Exodus 1:11) — is now maintained within Solomon’s empire.
10: ʾēlleh śārê haNeṣṣāḇîm (these are the chief officers/overseers) — niṣṣāḇ (H5324, Niphal of yāṣaḇ, standing/stationed) — the officers stationed over the work. 250 chief officers in Chronicles vs. 550 in 1 Kings 9:23 (a different accounting perhaps of the same or overlapping administrative tiers). The administrative numbers in Chronicles and Kings reflect different archival sources rather than editorial invention.
11: waYYaʿal šlōmōh ʾeT-bat-parʿōh — Solomon moved Pharaoh’s daughter. The reason given: kî ʾāmar lōʾ-tēšēḇ ʾiššāh lî bĕbêT dāwîḏ melek-yiśrāʾēl — ‘my wife must not live in the house of David king of Israel, for the places are holy that the ark of YHWH has entered.’ The holiness principle: the ark’s presence has consecrated David’s city with a grade of holiness incompatible with the residence of a Gentile wife. qāḏôš (holy) places that the ark entered — spatial holiness extending beyond the sanctuary proper to the entire city of David.
12: waYYaʿal šlōmōh ʿôlôT lîYHWH — Solomon offered burnt offerings. ʿôlāh (H5930, burnt offering/whole burnt offering) — entirely consumed on the altar, nothing returned to the offerer. The ʿôlāh is the offering of total devotion: the animal is wholly given to YHWH. ʿal miZBaḥ YHWH ʾăšer bānāh lipnê hāʾûlām (on the altar of YHWH that he built in front of the portico) — the exterior altar in front of the temple’s vestibule (ûlām).
13: ûḇidBar môšeh laSŠAbbātôT wĕlaḥŏdāšîm wĕlaMMôʿādôT šālôš pĕʿāmîm baSSānāh — the three annual pilgrimage festivals: Ḥag hamMaṣṣôT (Feast of Unleavened Bread/Passover), Šāḇûôt (Feast of Weeks/Pentecost), Sukkôt (Feast of Tabernacles). môʿēḏ (H4150, appointed time/season/festival) — the technical term for the liturgical calendar (Lev 23; Num 28-29). The festivals are characterized as according to midBār môšeh (the command of Moses) — Solomon’s worship is explicitly Mosaic, grounded in the Torah calendar.
14: waYYaʿămēd... mišmĕrôT haKKōhănîm — he appointed the priestly divisions (mišmĕrôT, H4931, watch/guard/division). The mišmĕrôT system — the rotating schedule of priestly divisions serving at the temple — was attributed to David (1 Chronicles 24:3-19). Solomon implements what David organized. The Levites are assigned to lĕhaLLēL ûlĕšārēT negDed haKKōhănîm (to praise and to minister before the priests) — the Levitical role as support-ministers to the priests, with their primary function being praise (haLLēL).
15: lōʾ-sārû (they did not deviate) — the perfect obedience of priests and Levites to the king’s commands (miṣwat hamMelek). sûr (H5493, to turn aside/deviate) is the verb of covenant-straying: its negation signals that the temple’s inaugural liturgical order was faithfully maintained. The detail about the treasuries (ʿal-hāʾôṣārôT) confirms that financial administration followed the same disciplined structure as ritual service.
16: waYYikkon kol-mĕlaʾḵet šĕlōmōh — all Solomon’s work was made firm/established. kûn (H3559, Niphal: to be established/prepared/made ready) — the same root used for the established throne (2 Samuel 7:16; 1 Chronicles 17:14). mikYôm mûSad bêT YHWH wĕʿad-šallĕmô (from the day the foundation of YHWH’s house was laid until its completion) — šālem (H7999, to be complete/finished/at peace) — the completion verb that shares its root with šālôm: the house is at peace/complete. waYYiSHlem bêT YHWH (and the house of YHWH was finished) — the Piel of šālem: made complete, brought to full integrity.
17: ʾāz hālaḵ šĕlōmōh lĕʿeṣyôn-geḇer wĕʾel-ʾêlôT — Solomon went to Ezion-geber and Eloth on the coast of the Red Sea. Ezion-geber (Tell el-Kheleifeh, at the head of the Gulf of Aqaba/Eilat) was Israel’s Red Sea port. ʾêlôT (Elath/Eilat) is the ancient port city. The Solomonic maritime enterprise requires this access to the Red Sea routes — the trade network that brought the gold of Ophir.
18: waYYišlaḥ-lô ḥûrām bĕyad-ʿăḇādāyw ʾŏniyyôT — Huram sent him ships crewed by his experienced sailors. ʿăḇādāyw (his servants/sailors) + yôdĕʿê hayYām (who knew/experienced the sea) — Phoenician maritime expertise supplementing the Israelite trade capacity. wAYāḇōʾû ʾôPīrāh (and they came to Ophir) — Ophir is the legendary gold source; its precise location is debated (East Africa, Arabia, or India). ʾarBaʿ mēʾôT waḥămiSSîm kikkar zāhāḇ (450 talents of gold — 1 Kings 10:11 says 420; the variant reflects different archival traditions).
